items.q: Does open source always mean free of charge?
items.correct: No - open source refers to access to the source code; companies can and do charge for open-source products, support and services.
items.misconception: Yes - open source and free of charge are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Yes - it is illegal to sell open-source software.
items.why: Two meanings of 'free' collide: freedom to view and modify code versus zero price. The licence guarantees the first, not the second.

items.q: Is hacking into a system an offence under the Data Protection Act?
items.correct: No - unauthorised access is an offence under the Computer Misuse Act 1990; the Data Protection Act governs how organisations handle personal data.
items.misconception: Yes - the Data Protection Act is the law that bans hacking.
items.random_wrong: Yes - hacking is only covered by copyright law.
items.why: Both laws involve data, so they swap. Match the actor and act: breaking in (CMA) versus mishandling data you legitimately hold (DPA).

items.q: When you buy proprietary software, do you own it?
items.correct: No - you buy a licence to use it under set conditions; the publisher keeps ownership of the software and its source code.
items.misconception: Yes - buying software makes it your property to copy and resell.
items.random_wrong: Yes - ownership transfers as soon as you install it.
items.why: Buying a boxed product feels like buying a chair. The EULA tells the real story: rights of use, not ownership - which is why copying for friends breaches it.

items.q: Open source software is software whose:
items.correct: Source code is available for anyone to view, modify and redistribute
items.misconception: Source code is a closely guarded secret
items.why: It's Source code is available for anyone to view, modify and redistribute. “Source code is a closely guarded secret” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Buy a licence that restricts copying and modification
items.misconception: Receive the full source code to edit
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Buy a licence that restricts copying and modification. “Receive the full source code to edit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is the best example of a CULTURAL impact of technology?
items.correct: Social media changing how people communicate and socialise
items.misconception: A virus deleting files on a server
items.random_wrong: A faster graphics card being released
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Social media changing how people communicate and socialise. “A virus deleting files on a server” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Under the Data Protection Act 2018, organisations must:
items.correct: Keep personal data accurate and secure, and use it lawfully and fairly
items.why: It's Keep personal data accurate and secure, and use it lawfully and fairly. “Publish all customer data online” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Computer Misuse Act passed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1990. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
